sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Rare Fruit Council of Australia and WANATCA archives, the Australian cocoa research program (RIRDC), the Northern Territory and Queensland governments, AgriFutures, and the International Cocoa Organisation.

core.intro: Cacao, the chocolate tree, is a true rainforest understorey species from the equatorial lowlands of the Americas, so wherever you try it in Australia the essentials are the same: constant warmth, high humidity, no cold or frost, shelter from wind, shade while the tree is young, and rich, moist, free draining soil. It is one of the most demanding fruit trees you can attempt here. The state sections below explain where it actually crops (in practice only the wet tropical far north of Queensland) and where it can only be kept alive under glass; the notes here apply anywhere warm and humid enough to grow it.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Cacao is divided into three broad groups. Criollo is the rare, fine flavoured, low bitterness type behind luxury chocolate, but it is delicate and disease prone and, with the fine flavour grades generally, makes up only a few percent of world production. Forastero is the hardy, vigorous bulk cocoa that supplies the great majority of the world crop. Trinitario is a Criollo by Forastero hybrid that aims to pair fine flavour with vigour, and it is the kind of cocoa grown in Australia. Australian plants are almost always sold as seedlings or hybrid seedlings rather than named grafted clones: the Australian research trials used hybrid seed from Papua New Guinea, and the one named selection you are likely to see offered is SG2, a compact, heavy bearing Papua New Guinea hybrid grown mainly in the far north. Two close relatives are sometimes sold alongside cacao and are not the true chocolate tree: mocambo (Theobroma bicolor) and cupuassu (Theobroma grandiflorum).

sections.heading: Climate: the make or break factor
sections.body: Climate is what decides whether cacao is worth attempting at all. It is an equatorial tree, naturally found in lowland rainforest within about fifteen to twenty degrees of the equator and usually below 300 metres in altitude. It wants a year round temperature of roughly 18 to 32 degrees, and growth, flowering and fruiting all fall away once the temperature drops below about 20 degrees. It will not tolerate frost or cold, and it cannot take a dry spell of more than about five weeks, needing 1500 to 2000 millimetres of well distributed rain and high humidity through the whole year. In Australia only the wet tropical coast of the far north comes close to this, which is why the state sections that follow are so different from one another.

sections.heading: Planting, shade and shelter
sections.body: Cacao is an understorey tree and starts life in deep shade, so it is planted out as a young seedling (usually three to six months old) under shade, with seedlings wanting more than half shade for at least their first six months. The shade does two jobs: it softens light a young tree cannot yet handle, and it gives the still, humid microclimate and wind shelter the tree needs, because its big leaves and brittle wood are easily damaged by wind. As the tree establishes you can let in more light, and in some countries mature cacao is grown in near full sun where water and shelter are good. Plant into deep, fertile, free draining soil (about pH 5.5 to 7.5) with plenty of organic matter, keep a thick mulch over the shallow roots, and never let it dry out or sit waterlogged. Left alone a tree passes ten metres, but in cultivation it is pruned to a pickable three to five metres (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Flowers on the trunk, and why fruit set is low
sections.body: Cacao flowers in one of the plant kingdom's odder ways: hundreds of tiny waxy flowers emerge straight from cushions on the trunk and main branches, a habit called cauliflory. The flowers are no bigger than pinheads and face downwards, far too small and intricate for bees, so they are pollinated almost entirely by minute midges (tiny flies of the family Ceratopogonidae such as Forcipomyia) that breed in damp leaf litter and rotting plant debris on the forest floor. Even with good midge activity only about one to five percent of flowers are pollinated and set a pod, and the tree then sheds many of the young pods it does set, a natural thinning called cherelle wilt. You can help by keeping the ground around the tree moist, shaded and littered with decaying mulch so the midges breed, and serious growers hand pollinate to lift the set. This is why a healthy, flowering tree can still carry only a modest crop.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: A pod takes about five to six months to ripen after the flower is pollinated, turning from green to yellow or from deep red to orange as it matures. Because the tree flowers more or less continuously, the harvest is spread across the whole year with one or two heavier flushes rather than a single short season. Cut ripe pods from the trunk with a knife or secateurs and leave the cushion intact, because pulling a pod off tears the bark and destroys the cushion that would have flowered again. Timing is not critical (a ripe pod can wait a week or two on the tree), but left too long the beans inside start to germinate or rot. Each pod holds about 20 to 50 beans embedded in a sweet, juicy white pulp.

sections.heading: From pod to chocolate: pulp, fermentation and drying
sections.body: Splitting a ripe pod does not give you chocolate. The white pulp around the beans is pleasant to suck straight from the pod, tangy and sweet, but the beans themselves have no chocolate flavour when fresh: that flavour is created by fermentation. Scoop the beans and their pulp into a box, basket or heap (a batch of at least 50 to 100 kilograms holds its heat best), cover it, and let it ferment for about five to seven days, turning it once or twice to aerate it. The pulp liquefies and drains away, the mass heats to around 50 degrees, and the beans change from purple to brown as the chocolate flavour precursors form. The fermented beans are then dried slowly to about six or seven percent moisture, traditionally in the sun. Only after roasting are they ground into the cocoa liquor and cocoa butter that become chocolate. Ferment within a few days of harvest, because the wet beans do not keep.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a healthy potted seedling or grafted tree from a nursery that knows tropical fruit, and only if you can give it genuine tropical warmth and humidity or a heated glasshouse. Cacao is sold mostly as seedlings, and hybrid seedlings bear young (about two to three years) even though they vary a little from tree to tree; the compact SG2 selection is the one to look for if you want a known, heavy bearing type. Check that what you are buying is the true chocolate tree, Theobroma cacao, rather than a relative such as mocambo or cupuassu, unless you specifically want those. The in stock table above shows the seedlings and named types on sale now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Can you really grow a chocolate tree in Australia?
faqs.a: Yes, but only in the right place. The one part of the country reliably warm and humid enough to crop cacao is the wet tropical coast of far north Queensland, where a small specialty cocoa industry has grown up. Everywhere else it is a glasshouse or pot plant, because it cannot survive a cold winter in the open, so in the southern states and even on the subtropical coast it is grown as a curiosity rather than for a crop.

faqs.q: How long does a cacao tree take to produce pods?
faqs.a: A hybrid seedling usually starts to flower and set its first pods in about two to three years, with a worthwhile crop a year or two later. That assumes the tree has what it needs, steady warmth above about 20 degrees, high humidity and the tiny midges that pollinate it, so a tree kept in a marginal climate or under glass may grow for years and never fruit.

faqs.q: Can I grow cacao in a pot or a greenhouse?
faqs.a: While it is young, yes, and in a cool climate a heated, humid glasshouse or a warm sunroom is the only way to keep one going. Use a large pot of rich, free draining mix, hold it above about 18 degrees and out of every draught, and mist it to keep the humidity up. Treat it as a handsome foliage plant, because without tropical warmth all year it is very unlikely to flower and fruit.

faqs.q: Do I need more than one cacao tree to get pods?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Unlike some tropical fruit, many cacao seedlings and hybrids will set pods on their own, so a single tree can crop. A few clones are self incompatible and do better with a different tree close by, and because so few flowers are ever pollinated, having more trees and plenty of pollinating midges about will always lift the harvest.

QLD.intro: Growing cacao in Queensland. The wet tropical coast of the far north is the only part of Australia where cacao is grown as a crop, and the country's small cocoa industry is concentrated there.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Cacao needs constant warmth, high humidity and year round moisture, conditions found in Australia only along the wet tropical coast of the far north, roughly from Cardwell up to the Daintree. An eight year government and industry research program, backed by the chocolate maker Cadbury, found that of all the sites trialled across northern Australia the far north Queensland sites performed best, giving acceptable yields (up to about three tonnes of dry bean per hectare) and good quality with no major pest or disease problems. Further south the cooler, drier subtropical south east of the state, including Brisbane, is marginal: a tree can be kept alive in a warm, sheltered spot, but the cooler, drier winters make reliable flowering and pod set unlikely.

sections.heading: Where cacao grows in Queensland
sections.body: The cacao country is the high rainfall coastal strip of the far north. The main districts run from the Daintree and Mossman north of Cairns, south through Babinda and the Innisfail and South Johnstone area, down to Tully, Mission Beach and Cardwell. Commercial plantings are concentrated around Mossman and Innisfail, and the state runs a cocoa research station at South Johnstone. The total planted area is still small, only around 30 to 35 hectares.

sections.heading: The Australian cocoa industry
sections.body: Australia's cocoa industry is young and deliberately small. It grew out of the research program of 1999 to 2007, after Cadbury sought a possible local supply, and the South Australian chocolate maker Haigh's produced the first Australian chocolate from far north Queensland beans. Because local growers cannot match the price of smallholder farms overseas, the industry aims squarely at premium, single origin and specialty chocolate, often paired with farm tours and on site chocolate making. Growers such as Charley's near Mission Beach and the Daintree Estates plantation near Mossman sell their own bean to bar chocolate, and an industry body now coordinates research and marketing.

sections.heading: Harvest window in Queensland
sections.body: Because cacao flowers almost all year, far north Queensland trees carry pods more or less continuously, with one or two heavier flushes rather than a single harvest season. Each pod ripens about five to six months after flowering, so picking is staggered, with growers walking the rows every week or two through the warmer, wetter part of the year to cut the pods as they colour up.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Queensland
sections.body: Here Australia has a real advantage. The far north Queensland trials ran into no major pest or disease problems, and the country is free of the witches broom, frosty pod and swollen shoot diseases that cripple cocoa across the Americas and Africa. The cocoa pod borer, the worst insect pest of cocoa in Asia, turned up on a far north Queensland plantation in 2011 but was eradicated by 2014 and is no longer present. The main thing a grower watches for is black pod, a Phytophthora rot that attacks pods in the wettest weather, along with rats and other animals that gnaw at ripening pods, so good airflow, prompt picking and netting or baiting keep losses down.

faqs.q: Can you grow a cacao tree in Brisbane or southeast Queensland?
faqs.a: You can keep the tree alive, but do not count on a crop. Brisbane and the subtropical south east are cooler and drier in winter than cacao likes, and below about 20 degrees it more or less stops growing and flowering, so a backyard tree in a warm, sheltered, frost free corner may grow happily yet set few or no pods. Dependable cropping really belongs to the humid tropical far north.

faqs.q: Which cacao should I plant in far north Queensland?
faqs.a: The proven performers in the far north are the Papua New Guinea hybrid seedlings used in the research trials and the compact SG2 selection grown in the cocoa districts. Buy a healthy seedling or grafted tree from a tropical nursery, give it shade and shelter while it is young, and pick a moist, mulched, midge friendly spot so the flowers actually set.

WA.intro: Growing cacao in Western Australia. Cacao sits beyond what Western Australia's climate can give it: even the tropical far north was trialled and grew poorly, and the south west cannot grow it outdoors at all.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Cacao demands steady equatorial warmth and humidity all year, with no cold, no frost and no long dry spell. Western Australia cannot offer that. The tropical far north is hot enough by day but has a long, harsh dry season, cooler dry season nights and very hot, dry summer days, none of which suit a rainforest tree. The south west, where most people garden, has a Mediterranean climate of dry summers and cool, wet, sometimes frosty winters that is completely wrong for cacao.

sections.heading: The Broome and Kununurra trials
sections.body: Western Australia did not just rule cacao out on paper: it was actually trialled here. The national research program planted a cocoa trial at Broome and further plantings at the Frank Wise Research Institute at Kununurra in the Ord. Both did badly. Growers struggled to get seed and seedlings to establish, and the young trees grew poorly, held back by low winter night temperatures, low winter humidity and high summer day temperatures, so the Western Australian part of the trial was discontinued early in the program. A parallel university study measured very low photosynthesis in the Kununurra trees compared with the Queensland sites, and no commercial cacao ever followed in the state.

sections.heading: Perth and the south west
sections.body: In Perth and the south west, cacao is a glasshouse plant at best. The dry summers, cool wet winters and occasional frost are far outside its range, and the Western Australian rare fruit community points to humidity, irrigation and minimum temperatures as the obstacles that defeat it here. A heated, humid glasshouse kept above about 18 degrees can keep a potted specimen alive, but a crop is not a realistic goal.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to Western Australia
sections.body: Western Australia's strict plant quarantine limits which interstate nurseries can post live plants into the state, and cacao is scarce in the trade in any case, so very few sellers can send one here. The in stock table above is filtered to sellers that can deliver to Western Australia.

faqs.q: Can I grow a cacao tree in Perth?
faqs.a: No, not outdoors. Perth's dry summers and cool, wet, sometimes frosty winters are a long way outside what this equatorial rainforest tree can survive, let alone fruit on. The only way to keep one is in a heated, humid glasshouse, and even then pods are unlikely. For a tropical flavour that actually grows in Perth, choose a hardier subtropical suited to the Mediterranean climate.

faqs.q: Has cacao ever been grown in Western Australia?
faqs.a: Only as a research trial, never as a crop. Test plantings in the tropical north were given up after the young trees failed to thrive, and no commercial cacao has ever followed. The tropical fruit that actually pays its way in far north Western Australia are the likes of mango and banana, which shrug off the long dry season that cacao cannot.

NSW.intro: Growing cacao in New South Wales. Cacao is a strictly equatorial tree, and even the warm far north coast of New South Wales is far too cool in winter for it, so here it is a heated glasshouse curiosity rather than a garden crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Cacao needs nights above about 20 degrees and is harmed by any cold, so New South Wales is well outside its range. The warmest part of the state, the far north coast, still has winter nights in the low teens and single figures, and frost reaches not far inland, while the rest of the state is colder again. An equatorial rainforest tree simply cannot pass a New South Wales winter in the open.

sections.heading: Where it might just survive
sections.body: If a cacao is going to survive anywhere in the state unprotected, it is in the warmest, most sheltered, frost free pockets of the far north coast, the Northern Rivers country around Byron, Lismore and the Tweed. Even there it is a fragile backyard curiosity that rarely if ever sets fruit, and a warm courtyard or a glasshouse gives it a better chance than open ground. Gardeners chasing a tropical fruit in northern New South Wales do far better with a hardier choice.

sections.heading: Growing one under cover, and what to grow instead
sections.body: To keep cacao alive away from the warm coast you need a heated, humid glasshouse or conservatory held above about 18 degrees, where it can be grown as a foliage specimen rather than a fruit tree. For a fruit that actually tastes of chocolate and crops in warm parts of New South Wales, the black sapote, sold as the chocolate pudding fruit, is a far easier subtropical tree, and bananas and jaboticaba are other hardier options.

faqs.q: Can I grow cacao on the New South Wales north coast?
faqs.a: Only under cover, really. Even the warm Northern Rivers has winters too cool for an equatorial tree to crop, so an outdoor cacao there is a gamble that usually ends in a sulking, fruitless tree, or a dead one after a cold snap. If you want to try, give it the warmest, most sheltered frost free corner you have, or grow it in a glasshouse.

faqs.q: What chocolate flavoured fruit can I actually grow in New South Wales instead?
faqs.a: Plant a black sapote, often sold as the chocolate pudding fruit. It is a subtropical tree that crops on the warm New South Wales coast, and its rich brown flesh really does taste like chocolate mousse. It asks for none of the heated, humid coddling that a cacao demands, which makes it the sensible chocolate fix for a garden here.

VIC.intro: Growing cacao in Victoria. Victoria is far too cold for cacao: the tree is killed by frost and set back by any cool spell, so here it is only ever a heated glasshouse specimen, never a crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Cacao wants warm, humid days of roughly 25 to 30 degrees and nights that stay above about 20 degrees, every month of the year, and it dies in frost. Victoria's cold, frosty winters and mild, changeable summers are nowhere near this, so there is no part of the state where cacao can be grown outdoors. The cool nights of a Melbourne summer alone would stall it, well before winter frost finished it off.

sections.heading: Growing one under glass
sections.body: The only way to keep a cacao in Victoria is in a warm, bright, humid glasshouse or heated conservatory held above about 18 degrees, the kind of tropical house found in botanic gardens, and even then it is grown for its handsome foliage and curious trunk flowers rather than for a crop. It is demanding and rarely rewarding. For fruit you can actually grow and eat in Victoria, hardier subtropicals such as feijoa and tamarillo are a far better use of a warm, sheltered Melbourne garden.

faqs.q: Can I grow a cacao tree outdoors in Melbourne?
faqs.a: No. Frost and cold winters kill cacao, and even the cool nights of a Melbourne summer are enough to stall it, so it cannot live outdoors anywhere in Victoria. The only way to keep one going is under heated, humid glass, and even then it almost never fruits this far south.

faqs.q: Is there a hardier chocolate plant for Victoria?
faqs.a: Cacao itself will not crop in Victoria, though a potted tree under glass can be a talking point. For something you can actually harvest, skip the chocolate tree and plant a hardier subtropical: a feijoa or a tamarillo will fruit in a warm, sheltered Victorian garden, giving you a taste of the exotic without the heated glasshouse cacao would need.
